factors.label: Judge eggs on biomarkers (LDL) vs hard CVD events
factors.rationale: Weights hard clinical endpoints from large pooled cohorts.

provenance.quote: higher risk of incident cardiovascular disease and all-cause mortality
did not adversely affect the lipid profile
carotid plaque area increased with egg yolk consumption
The level of evidence was very weak for all the significant associations, including higher LDL cholesterol, while no association was found for cardiovascular outcomes.

factors.label: Discount for healthy-user / dietary-pattern confounding
factors.rationale: Claims the association survives confounding adjustment.

provenance.quote: associations were not modified by adjustment for dietary quality
associations were not significant after adjustment for lifestyle and dietary factors

factors.label: Population/context dependence (who is eating eggs)
factors.rationale: A different population and background diet flips the direction of association.

provenance.quote: in this Chinese population, moderate egg consumption
The wide range of outcomes, with substantial variability and high heterogeneity, indicated a lack of robust evidence.

factors.label: Subgroups (diabetics / hyper-responders) differ
factors.rationale: Overall null hides a diabetic subgroup signal — the crux of 'depends on who'.

provenance.quote: among diabetic subjects, egg consumption was associated with increased risk
a subgroup analysis showed higher risk of coronary heart disease among diabetic patients
associated with higher CVD risk among people with diabetes

factors.label: Discount for industry funding
factors.rationale: Industry-funded RCT concluding eggs are safe for diabetics.

provenance.quote: funded by the Australian Egg Corporation
supported by the Egg Nutrition Center
industry-funded studies were far more likely to report favorable conclusions
supported by the American Egg Board's Egg Nutrition Center
funded by the Egg Nutrition Center

sources.title: Zhong et al. — Associations of dietary cholesterol/egg consumption with CVD and mortality (JAMA)

position.quote: each additional 300 mg of dietary cholesterol per day was significantly associated with higher risk of incident CVD and all-cause mortality; each half an egg per day was associated with higher risk

sources.title: Zhuang et al. — Egg & cholesterol consumption and mortality (NIH-AARP, PLOS Medicine)

position.quote: higher egg and dietary-cholesterol intake was associated with higher cardiovascular disease mortality and incident CVD

fundingDetails: National Key Research and Development Program of China

sources.title: Drouin-Chartier et al. — Egg consumption and risk of cardiovascular disease: three cohorts + meta-analysis (BMJ)

position.quote: Title: Egg consumption and risk of cardiovascular disease: three large prospective US cohort studies, systematic review, and updated meta-analysis URL Source: https://www.bmj.com/content/368/bmj.m513 Published Time: 2020-03-04 Markdown Content: 1.

sources.title: Dehghan et al. — Egg consumption and CVD/mortality in 50 countries (PURE)
sources.venue: The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition

position.quote: egg intake was not significantly associated with blood lipids, mortality, or major cardiovascular disease events

sources.title: Qin et al. — Egg consumption and cardiovascular disease (China Kadoorie Biobank)

position.quote: daily egg consumption was associated with a lower risk of cardiovascular disease

sources.title: Hu et al. — A prospective study of egg consumption and risk of cardiovascular disease (JAMA)

position.quote: consumption of up to one egg per day is unlikely to have substantial overall impact on the risk of CHD... among diabetic subjects, egg consumption was associated with increased risk

sources.title: Rong et al. — Egg consumption and risk of CHD and stroke: dose-response meta-analysis (BMJ)

position.quote: higher consumption of eggs was not associated with increased risk of coronary heart disease or stroke

sources.title: Fuller et al. (DIABEGG) — High vs low egg diet in type 2 diabetes: randomized trial
sources.venue: The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition

position.quote: a high-egg diet did not adversely affect the lipid profile of people with type 2 diabetes... eggs can be included in the diet

sources.title: Blesso & Fernandez — Whole egg consumption improves lipoprotein profiles (feeding trial)

position.quote: whole egg consumption led to increases in HDL cholesterol and improvements in the lipoprotein profile

sources.title: Carson et al. — Dietary cholesterol and cardiovascular risk: an AHA science advisory

position.quote: healthy individuals can include up to one egg per day within a heart-healthy dietary pattern

sources.title: Barnard et al. — Industry Funding and Cholesterol Research: A Systematic Review

position.quote: dietary cholesterol, including that from eggs, raises LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular disease risk — a signal that industry-funded studies tend to obscure

fundingDetails: Conflict disclosed: lead author was uncompensated president of the Physicians Committee for Responsible Medicine, which advocates plant-based diets

sources.title: Djoussé & Gaziano — Egg consumption and risk of heart failure (Physicians' Health Study)

position.quote: consumption of eggs at a rate of 1 or more per day was associated with increased risk of heart failure

sources.title: Spence, Jenkins & Davignon — Egg yolk consumption and carotid plaque

position.quote: carotid plaque area increased with egg yolk consumption in a pattern similar to that with smoking

sources.title: Alexander et al. — Meta-analysis of egg consumption and risk of CHD and stroke
sources.venue: Journal of the American College of Nutrition

position.quote: egg consumption was not associated with the risk of coronary heart disease or stroke

sources.title: Tran et al. — Egg consumption and cardiovascular disease: a meta-analysis
sources.venue: Handbook of Egg Science and Technology

position.quote: egg consumption was not associated with increased risk of cardiovascular disease

sources.title: Virtanen et al. — Egg consumption and risk of coronary artery disease (Kuopio, KIHD)
sources.venue: The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition

position.quote: egg intake was not associated with the risk of coronary artery disease, even among APOE4 carriers

sources.title: Berger et al. — Dietary cholesterol and cardiovascular disease: a systematic review and meta-analysis
sources.venue: The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition

position.quote: dietary cholesterol was not statistically significantly associated with any coronary artery disease or stroke outcome

fundingDetails: American Egg Board, Egg Nutrition Center

sources.title: Shin et al. — Egg consumption in relation to cardiovascular disease and diabetes: a meta-analysis
sources.venue: The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition

position.quote: egg consumption was not associated with CVD overall, but was associated with higher CVD risk among people with diabetes

sources.title: Larsson et al. — Egg consumption and risk of heart failure, myocardial infarction and stroke (Swedish cohorts)
sources.venue: The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition

position.quote: egg consumption was not associated with myocardial infarction or stroke, but higher consumption was associated with heart failure risk

fundingDetails: Swedish Research Council for Health, Working Life and Welfare

sources.title: Godos et al. — Egg consumption and cardiovascular risk: a dose-response meta-analysis of prospective cohort studies

position.quote: There is no conclusive evidence on the role of egg in CVD risk; possible protection at moderate intake and increased heart-failure risk at high intake both require stronger evidence.

sources.title: Formisano et al. — Effect of egg consumption on health outcomes: an updated umbrella review

position.quote: No evidence of association was found among all cardiovascular outcomes and all-cause mortality risk between high vs. low egg consumption.
